summary: DeVault lays out a set of guiding principles for writing disciplined C code, arguing that C's power demands careful, deliberate practice rather than cleverness. He advocates for maintainability over performance, transparency over abstraction, and strict adherence to standard features over GNU/gcc extensions. The post covers code organization, dangerous pattern avoidance, disciplined version control, rigorous testing, and learning from mistakes. He draws an analogy between C programming and gun handling—both are powerful tools requiring respect and discipline. Throughout, he maintains that C remains one of the best programming languages when used correctly, and that the real danger lies not in the language but in undisciplined programmers.
key_points: Maintainability should always be prioritized over cleverness and performance—write code as if last year's version of yourself will maintain it
Avoid all forms of 'magic' including macros, typedefs that hide pointers, and complex abstractions—C's transparency is its greatest strength
Never assume GNU/Linux/gcc/glibc/x86—use only standard C features and isolate platform-specific code behind clean interfaces
Treat mistakes as process failures, not individual failures—fix the bug, then fix the workflow that allowed it to happen
Disciplined version control with thoughtful commit messages is as important as disciplined code
money_quotes: Do not be clever where cleverness is not required. Instead, seek out the simplest and most understandable solution that meets the requirements.
Write code as if the person maintaining it was you, circa last year.
One of C's greatest advantages is its transparency and simplicity. This should be embraced, not subverted.
Writing C is sometimes like handling a gun. Guns are important tools, but accidents with them can be very bad.
GNU is a blight on this Earth, do not let it infect your code.
When you see some stupid code, it's nice to know what the bastard was thinking at the time, especially when the bastard in question was you.
First, fix the bug. Then, fix the real bug: your process allowed this mistake to happen.
key_insight: C's power and simplicity are assets only when paired with strict discipline—the language won't protect you from yourself, so your processes, habits, and taste must do the protecting instead.
